infobox.value: or **flag of São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro** (English: flag of Saint Sebastian of Rio de Janeiro) consists of a white rectangle with a blue [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") behind the coat of arms of the city of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), which is displayed red in the center. Its basic design has changed little since it was adopted on July 8, 1908, except during period from 1960 to 1975 when the municipality of Rio de Janeiro was known as [Guanabara State](/wiki/Guanabara_State "Guanabara State"). It resembles the flags of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)") (before it changed) and the [Russian Navy](/wiki/Russian_Navy "Russian Navy") (with some changes).[[1]](#cite_note-crwflags-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

For the flag of the state, see [Flag of Rio de Janeiro (state)](/wiki/Flag_of_Rio_de_Janeiro_(state) "Flag of Rio de Janeiro (state)").

São Sebastião  
do Rio de Janeiro

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
|  | |
| [Use](/wiki/Vexillological_symbol "Vexillological symbol") | [Civil](/wiki/Civil_flag "Civil flag") and [state flag](/wiki/State_flag "State flag") |
| Proportion | 7:10 |
| Adopted | July 8, 1908 |
| Design | A white rectangle with a blue saltire behind the city's coat of arms displayed red in the center |
|  | |

The **flag of the City of Rio de Janeiro** or **flag of São Sebastião do Rio de Janeiro** (English: flag of Saint Sebastian of Rio de Janeiro) consists of a white rectangle with a blue [saltire](/wiki/Saltire "Saltire") behind the coat of arms of the city of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), which is displayed red in the center. Its basic design has changed little since it was adopted on July 8, 1908, except during period from 1960 to 1975 when the municipality of Rio de Janeiro was known as [Guanabara State](/wiki/Guanabara_State "Guanabara State"). It resembles the flags of [Galicia](/wiki/Galicia_(Spain) "Galicia (Spain)") (before it changed) and the [Russian Navy](/wiki/Russian_Navy "Russian Navy") (with some changes).[[1]](#cite_note-crwflags-1)

## Composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flag_of_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Composition")]

The flag of the City of Rio de Janeiro is a white field with two blue stripes, placed diagonally, forming a band and a bar (that is, in the shape of a saltire, or a St. Andrew's cross). On the crossing of the band and the bar, the city's coat of arms—a sixth in proportion to the total field—appears in red. The city's coat of arms has the following heraldic composition: on a red field, there are three silver arrows surmounted by a silver [armillary sphere](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere"), and over all a silver [Phrygian cap](/wiki/Phrygian_cap "Phrygian cap"). Supporting the shield are two red dolphins, holding at their right a laurel branch and at their left an oak branch. The crest of the shield is a [mural crown](/wiki/Mural_crown "Mural crown") of five towers, also red.[[1]](#cite_note-crwflags-1)

The [tinctures](/wiki/Tincture_(heraldry) "Tincture (heraldry)"), or heraldic colors, of the coat of arms on this shield are altered from the traditional coat of arms, which has a blue field, golden arrows and sphere, and a red Phrygian cap. The dolphins are normally silver, the leaves they hold green, and the mural crown gold.[[1]](#cite_note-crwflags-1)

The blue and white of the flag symbolize the Portuguese origin of the city, as they are the traditional colors of the [Portuguese monarchy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal"), adopted since the creation of the [County of Portugal](/wiki/County_of_Portugal "County of Portugal") in 1097 (it was only after the proclamation of the Portuguese Republic on October 5, 1910 that Portugal began to use the colors dark green and red on [their flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Portugal "Flag of Portugal")). The color red on the flag of Rio de Janeiro symbolizes the blood shed by [Saint Sebastian](/wiki/Saint_Sebastian "Saint Sebastian"), the saintly patron of the city, as well as the blood shed by the city's founder, [Estácio de Sá](/wiki/Est%C3%A1cio_de_S%C3%A1 "Estácio de Sá"), and the early settlers of Rio de Janeiro.
